facts: Earth's orbit around the Sun takes one year, and it is the tilt of Earth's axis, not the distance from the Sun, that causes the seasons because different hemispheres receive more direct sunlight at different times.
The Moon orbits Earth about once every 29.5 days, which is why its appearance changes from new to full and back over the course of a month.
Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward each other; it keeps planets in orbit around the Sun and moons around planets, and without it objects would fly off in straight lines.
Stars are giant balls of hot gas that produce their own light; the Sun is a medium-sized star, and other stars look small only because they are much farther away from Earth.
A galaxy is a huge collection of stars, gas, and dust held together by gravity; our solar system is just one tiny part of the Milky Way galaxy, which contains billions of stars.

Earth's axis is an imaginary line through the North and South Poles that is tilted at about 23.5 degrees, and this tilt causes the Sun's path across the sky to change height and length throughout the year, creating seasons.
misconceptions: Students believe Earth's seasons happen because Earth is closer to the Sun in summer and farther in winter, not because of the tilt of its axis.
Students think that gravity is a force that only exists on Earth and not in space, so they assume planets and stars float without any force holding them in orbit.
Students often confuse a galaxy and a solar system, thinking that our solar system is the entire galaxy or that galaxies are just groups of planets orbiting a single star.
